title: Hypotonia and Neuromuscular Disease in the Neonate

blocks.text:
ทำไม neonatologist ต้องเข้าใจ Hypotonia and Neuromuscular Disease?
Floppy infant คือ consult ที่เจอได้ทุกวันใน NICU — แต่ hypotonia ไม่เท่ากัน neuromuscular disease. (1) Localization คือกุญแจ: brain hypotonia (weak without proportionate weakness) vs peripheral hypotonia (weak WITH profound weakness) — ต่างกันทั้ง workup และ prognosis. (2) SMA type I เปลี่ยนชะตากรรมแล้ว: ก่อน 2017 = fatal before 1 yr; ปัจจุบัน FDA-approved 3 drugs (nusinersen, onasemnogene abeparvovec, risdiplam) และ newborn screening ใน 44 states ทำให้ presymptomatic treatment เป็นไปได้. (3) Congenital myotonic dystrophy: ลืมถามประวัติแม่ = ลืม diagnose — แม่ mild/asymptomatic ได้; ทารกอาจต้องใช้ ventilator นาน; >30 days ventilation = high mortality. (4) Central core disease = malignant hyperthermia risk: RYR1 gene mutation → สิ่งที่ดูเหมือน benign myopathy → triggered MH by volatile agents during surgery → fatal. (5) Transient neonatal myasthenia: แม่ MG ใน remission ก็ให้ได้; ทารกหาย spontaneously <2 months แต่ถ้าไม่รู้ = ทารกอาจ die from respiratory failure. เราต้องรู้เพื่อ recognize, localize, treat.
เป้าหมายการเรียนรู้วันนี้
อธิบาย examination ของ neonatal tone: normal term infant posture, pull-to-sit (head lag), vertical/horizontal suspension — signs ของ hypotonia
จำแนก central vs peripheral hypotonia: tone vs weakness, DTR, facial diplegia, tongue fasciculations, muscle bulk — ใช้ localize level ของ lesion
อธิบาย SMA type I (Werdnig-Hoffmann): SMN1 gene, phenotype, EMG/nerve conduction, 3 FDA-approved drugs, newborn screening rationale
ระบุ SMA variants ที่สำคัญใน neonates: SMA type 0, SMARD1, arthrogryposis multiplex congenita (neurogenic) — genetic basis
อธิบาย hereditary motor and sensory neuropathies: congenital hypomyelinating neuropathy, Dejerine-Sottas — NCV หนักข้อ <10 m/s
ระบุ familial dysautonomia (HSAN III): 5 cardinal criteria, ethnic group, IKBKAP gene — diagnosis ใน newborn period
จำแนก neuromuscular junction disorders: transient neonatal MG (20% of maternal MG), congenital myasthenic syndromes, infantile botulism, hypermagnesemia, aminoglycosides
อธิบาย congenital muscular dystrophy: merosin-deficient CMD (LAMA2), Walker-Warburg (POMT1/POMT2), MEB (POMGnT1), Fukuyama (FKTN) — overlap กับ Ch58 cobblestone lissencephaly
อธิบาย congenital myotonic dystrophy (DM1): CTG repeat expansion >1000, maternal transmission, facial diplegia, tent-shaped upper lip, respiratory failure
ระบุ 4 major congenital myopathies: nemaline (rod bodies, Gomori trichrome), central core disease (RYR1 → MH!), multi-minicore (SEPN1/RYR1), myotubular (X-linked, MTM1)
อธิบาย Pompe disease (GSD II): acid maltase deficiency, cardiomegaly, giant QRS, enzyme replacement therapy (GAA)
ระบุ bedside workup สำหรับ floppy infant: stepwise approach จาก maternal history → examination → investigation ladder
1. Assessment ของ Neonatal Tone
1.1 Normal Term Infant Tone
Supine: active movement of flexed limbs; hips flexed 70–90°, abducted ~10–20°; popliteal angle ~90° with passive extension
Pull-to-sit: slight head lag only; on reaching sitting position head wobbles in midline briefly
Vertical suspension: sits 'in air' — normal shoulder girdle tone prevents slipping through examiner's hands; legs flexed
Horizontal suspension: limbs flexed, back straight, head maintained in midline briefly
Premature infant: decreasing flexor tone with decreasing gestational age — normal findings must be age-adjusted
1.2 Hypotonic Infant Findings
Frog-like supine posture: hips abducted + abnormal extension of limbs
Pull-to-sit: head lag with no compensation on reaching sitting position
Vertical: slips through examiner's hands (↓ shoulder girdle tone); legs more extended than flexed
Horizontal: back hangs over examiner's hand; head and limbs hang loosely
1.3 Diagnosis Framework — Central vs Peripheral

Central vs Peripheral Hypotonia — Key Distinguishing Features
Central (upper motor neuron / brain): tone ↓ disproportionately more than weakness; limbs retain some antigravity power; abnormal primitive reflexes; poor arousal; fisting ('cortical thumb'); ↑ DTRs or clonus (sustained); abnormal eye movements / gaze; seizures. Brain imaging abnormal. CK normal. Peripheral (LMN: anterior horn cell, nerve, NMJ, muscle): profound weakness = tone ↓; ↓ or absent DTRs; tongue fasciculations (anterior horn cell); ptosis/ophthalmoplegia (NMJ); facial diplegia; poor suck/swallow; high CK (myopathy). Important caveat: near-total asphyxia → absent reflexes despite central origin (brainstem/upper cord); CMD/congenital myotonic dystrophy can have BOTH central + peripheral involvement. Always cross-check.
Medications from mother: magnesium sulfate → presynaptic block of ACh release; aminoglycosides; benzodiazepines — always check maternal meds as first step before launching genetic workup
Metabolic/systemic illness: sepsis, organ failure, hypoglycemia, metabolic crisis → all cause hypotonia as non-specific feature; rule out FIRST
2. Spinal Muscular Atrophy (SMA)
2.1 SMA Type I — Werdnig-Hoffmann Disease
Autosomal recessive: degeneration of anterior horn cells + lower brainstem motor nuclei

Genetics: 95% have homozygous deletion of exon 7 of SMN1 gene on chromosome 5q13; telomeric copy = SMN1 (functional); centromeric copy = SMN2 (single nucleotide polymorphism 840C>T → mostly truncated/nonfunctional protein)
SMN2 copy number: direct relationship with age of onset; SMA type 0 (neonatal/prenatal onset) = lowest SMN2 copies; more copies → later/milder phenotype (type II, III, IV)

Clinical features (type I/0): severe symmetric flaccid paralysis; proximal > distal; lower > upper limbs; absent DTRs; alert expression with furrowed brow + normal eye movements (upper cranial nerves spared); weak cry; poor suck/swallow; tongue fasciculations; pooling of secretions; relative diaphragm sparing → bell-shaped chest (intercostal weakness > diaphragm); cardiac muscle NOT affected
EMG/NCS: normal/slightly ↓ motor NCV; normal sensory action potentials; EMG: fibrillations + positive sharp waves; ↑ mean duration + amplitude MUPs (some polyphasic); CK: normal or slightly ↑

Muscle biopsy: groups of circular atrophic type 1 and 2 fibers interspersed with hypertrophied type 1 fibers (3–4× normal size = reinnervated); neonatal period may show only widespread atrophy → biopsy often not needed with genetic testing
Natural history without treatment: severe neonatal SMA → rarely survives >1 year; severity at onset determines outcome; early onset = greater weakness
2.2 FDA-Approved Treatments — Game Changer

SMA Therapies — 3 FDA-Approved Drugs (2017–2021)
1. Nusinersen (FDA December 2017): antisense oligonucleotide; modifies pre-mRNA splicing of SMN2 → ↑ full-length SMN protein production. Phase 3 ENDEAR trial: 51% nusinersen vs 0% sham had motor-milestone response; statistically significant event-free survival (Finkel NEJM 2017;377:1723). Intrathecal injection. 2. Onasemnogene abeparvovec / Zolgensma (FDA 2020): single IV infusion of AAV vector containing SMN1 gene. Gene replacement. Patients treated EARLIEST showed most improvement; some achieved ability to walk (Mendell NEJM 2017;377:1713). 3. Risdiplam (FDA 2020–2021): oral small molecule; acts on SMN2 pre-mRNA splicing → ↑ functional SMN protein. FIREFISH trial: risdiplam infants vs historical controls showed superior motor milestones (Darras NEJM 2021;385:427). Principle: earlier = better outcomes. → led to newborn screening.
2.3 Newborn Screening
As of March 2022: 44 US states screen for SMA; screening anticipated to identify 364 babies, prevent ~30 deaths, and have 50 infants avoid assisted ventilation (Jdrzejowska Degener Neurol 2020)
Screening based on SMN1 gene deletion detection; presymptomatic treatment → dramatically better outcomes than symptomatic treatment
Resource: www.curesma.org
2.4 SMA Variants and Related Disorders
SMA with pontocerebellar hypoplasia: additional brainstem/cerebellar involvement; worse prognosis
X-linked SMA with arthrogryposis (UBE1 gene): X-linked inheritance; articular contractures
SMARD1 (SMA with respiratory distress): IGHMBP2 gene; presents in neonates with diaphragm paralysis + congenital contractures; distinguished from SMA I by diaphragm weakness > intercostal weakness

Arthrogryposis multiplex congenita (AMC): heterogeneous group; congenital multi-joint contractures from ↓ fetal movement; neurogenic, myopathic, genetic, connective tissue causes; subgroup allelic with SMA I (SMN deletion found)

Traumatic cervical SCI: SMA mimic; alert infant, no cranial nerve signs; flaccid areflexic paralysis ± asymmetric; clues: bruising, bladder distention, priapism, no sweating below level; sensory testing — facial grimace to prick but no response below neck
Severe HI injury: areflexic flaccid paralysis from spinal motor neuron death; BUT encephalopathy + multisystem organ damage present
3. Hereditary Neuropathies
3.1 Hereditary Motor and Sensory Neuropathies (HMSN)
Spectrum: Charcot-Marie-Tooth type 1 (demyelinating), CMT type 2 (axonal), hereditary neuropathy with liability to pressure palsies, Dejerine-Sottas syndrome, congenital hypomyelinating neuropathy
Inheritance: autosomal dominant, autosomal recessive, or X-linked depending on gene

Neonatal presentation (congenital hypomyelinating neuropathy + Dejerine-Sottas): weak + hypotonic + areflexic; generalized but ↑ distal weakness; arthrogryposis in severe forms; swallowing + respiratory difficulties; sometimes facial weakness; extraocular movements usually normal
Hallmark: markedly ↓ motor NCV < 10 m/s; ↑ CSF protein; sural nerve biopsy: hypomyelination ± atypical onion-bulb (neonatal period = less onion-bulb); severe = complete absence of myelin

Key genes: Schwann cell genes (EGR2, PMP22, MPZ, CX32, PRX); neuronal (NEFL); both Schwann + neuronal (MTMR2); same gene → different phenotypes (CMT1, Dejerine-Sottas, congenital hypomyelinating neuropathy)
Prognosis: depends on initial degree of weakness; some cases show improvement in strength; molecular genetic testing makes nerve biopsy rarely needed
3.2 Hereditary Sensory and Autonomic Neuropathies (HSAN)

rows: Hereditary sensory radicular neuropathy; autosomal dominant; late-onset
Ashkenazi Jewish; IKBKAP gene (99%); chr 9q31-q33; AR; most important neonatal type
Congenital insensitivity to pain with anhidrosis; self-mutilation
Congenital insensitivity to pain with partial anhidrosis

blocks.text:
Familial Dysautonomia (HSAN III / Riley-Day Syndrome) — 5 Cardinal Criteria
Ethnic group: Ashkenazi Jewish (high carrier rate); IKBKAP gene mutation on chromosome 9q31-q33 accounts for 99% of cases. 5 cardinal criteria for clinical diagnosis: (1) absence of overflow tears; (2) absence of lingual fungiform papillae; (3) depressed patellar reflexes; (4) lack of axonal flare reaction after intradermal histamine injection; (5) Ashkenazi Jewish extraction. Additional features: hypotonia, labile temperature/blood pressure, breath holding, pallor, poor feeding, FTT, vomiting, loose stools, irritability. Management: supportive; survival improved with modern therapy; slow progression into adulthood; cognition may be impaired in some forms.

Congenital central hypoventilation syndrome (CCHS): in DDx of autonomic neuropathy in neonate; PHOX2B gene mutation; hypotonia, ↓ DTRs, hypoventilation with absent response to hypercarbia; autonomic dysfunction; risk for Hirschsprung disease + neural crest tumors; clinical diagnosis + genetic confirmation
4. Neuromuscular Junction Disorders
4.1 Transient Neonatal Myasthenia Gravis
Autoimmune MG: AChR antibodies (80%), muscle-specific kinase antibodies (remaining) → abnormal neuromuscular transmission

Incidence: only 20% of infants born to mothers with MG develop symptoms; maternal disease may be active, in remission, or not yet clinically apparent; no correlation with maternal antibody titer or disease severity; inverse relationship between maternal disease duration and incidence of neonatal MG

Onset: within a few hours to 72 hours of birth; generalized weakness/hypotonia; bulbar (poor suck/swallow, weak cry); facial diplegia; ptosis/ophthalmoplegia less common; respiratory failure occasionally
DTRs: NORMAL — key differentiator from other NMJ and LMN disorders

Diagnosis: neostigmine methylsulfate IM/SC 0.04 mg/kg; effect in 15 min, lasts 1–3 hours; must show unequivocal objective response (ventilation, oxygenation, sucking); NOT edrophonium in neonates — risk of respiratory arrest; repetitive nerve stimulation: ≥10% decrement of 5th vs 1st response (corrected by cholinesterase inhibitor = positive)

Treatment: neostigmine IM/SC 0.04 mg/kg q4–6h, 30 min before meals; oral dose ~10× IM dose (45 min before feeding); tube feeding + ventilatory support if needed; atropine for muscarinic side effects (diarrhea, ↑ secretions)
Duration: tube feeding/ventilation rarely needed >1–2 weeks; average treatment duration 4 weeks; 90% recover within 2 months
4.2 Congenital Myasthenic Syndromes (CMS)
Antibody-NEGATIVE: genetic (inherited) defects of NMJ — presynaptic, synaptic, or postsynaptic

rows: Choline acetyltransferase deficiency; paucity of synaptic vesicles; similar to Eaton-Lambert
Endplate acetylcholinesterase deficiency — refractory to/worsened by cholinesterase inhibitors!
AChR subunit defect (ptosis + ophthalmoplegia); rapsyn mutation (more severe); kinetic AChR abnormalities (slow/fast channel)

blocks.text: Clinical: ptosis usually present; ± ophthalmoplegia; bulbar palsy; respiratory weakness; fluctuating generalized hypotonia; episodic life-threatening apnea; exacerbations with activity, fever, stress; improve with age but sudden infant death risk
Diagnosis: decremental response at 2 Hz repetitive nerve stimulation (may need 10-Hz stimulation for some presynaptic defects); some forms cholinesterase inhibitor response variable
Endplate AChE deficiency: refractory or WORSENED by cholinesterase inhibitors — critical to identify before treating
4.3 Infantile Botulism
Age: 2 weeks – 6 months; unlike adult botulism (exotoxin ingestion), infantile botulism = intestinal colonization by C. botulinum → in situ toxin production
Toxin: presynaptic cholinergic blockage → autonomic + skeletal + smooth muscle dysfunction

Clinical spectrum: mild (constipation + hypotonia) → unexpected death; classic: constipation + poor feeding → progressive descending hypotonic weakness + loss of DTRs; cranial nerve dysfunction (pupillary paralysis + ptosis); illness lasts 1–2 months; small relapse rate
EMG/NCS: incremental response at high-frequency (20–50 Hz) repetitive nerve stimulation; short-duration low-amplitude MUPs; diagnosis confirmed by C. botulinum isolation from stool
Treatment: human-derived botulism immunoglobulin (BabyBIG) promptly → significantly ↓ duration of hospital stay and recovery time (Rao MMWR 2021;70:1); supportive management otherwise
4.4 Toxic NMJ Disorders

Hypermagnesemia: IV maternal MgSO4 → ↑ serum Mg in infant → presynaptic failure of ACh release by blocking Ca²⁺ release; profound weakness + areflexia + respiratory dysfunction + CNS (stupor) + smooth muscle (ileus); worsened by hypocalcemia
Aminoglycosides: especially with concomitant neuromuscular blockers → prolonged weakness from presynaptic block; bladder/bowel/pupillary function affected; also worsened by hypocalcemia
5. Congenital Muscular Dystrophy (CMD)
5.1 General Features

Autosomal recessive; weakness + hypotonia AT BIRTH + dystrophic muscle biopsy: endomysial + perimysial connective tissue proliferation, replacement by fat, fiber size variation; little necrosis/regeneration
Generalized weakness including face; bulbar and respiratory variable but can be severe; CK elevated in some types; arthrogryposis common
Division: classic (nonsyndromic, normal cognition) vs syndromic (brain malformations + mental retardation)
5.2 Classic CMD — Merosin-Deficient (MDC1A)

Most common CMD (~50%): merosin = heavy alpha-2 chain of laminin 2 (protein); links extracellular matrix to dystrophin-associated glycoproteins → critical for myogenesis + membrane stability; also expressed in Schwann cells, trophoblasts, cerebral blood vessels
LAMA2 gene mutations (chromosome 6q): sequence analysis available; diagnosis also by muscle biopsy with complete absence of merosin on immunocytochemical staining

Clinical: marked neonatal hypotonia + generalized weakness + atrophy; facial weakness (eye muscles spared); early contractures; chest wall → respiratory difficulties; cognition usually unaffected; peripheral neuropathy (absent Schwann cell merosin); white matter changes on T2 MRI (best seen after 6 months); epilepsy in 1/3
5.3 Syndromic CMD — Dystroglycanopathies

Syndromic CMD — Walker-Warburg / MEB / Fukuyama (O-Glycosylation Defects)
Shared mechanism: defective O-glycosylation of alpha-dystroglycan → ↓ binding to laminin (extracellular) and beta-dystroglycan (transmembrane) → muscle membrane + brain migration defects. Same mechanism as type II cobblestone lissencephaly (Ch58 Day 155 = same as Ch58 content in Day 150 lissencephaly section). Walker-Warburg (POMT1/POMT2): most severe; cobblestone lissencephaly + CC agenesis + cerebellar hypoplasia + hydrocephalus + encephalocele; lethal within first postnatal months; severe ocular involvement (cataracts, microphthalmia, retinal dysplasia). Muscle-eye-brain disease (POMGnT1): cobblestone lissencephaly + myopia + glaucoma + retinal hypoplasia + dysgenesis CC; prolonged survival but ↑ MR; giant-amplitude VEPs by 2 yr. Fukuyama CMD (FKTN, chromosome 9q31): almost exclusively Japanese; milder brain (pachygyria/polymicrogyria with areas of normal gyri); minor ocular involvement; seizures + MR; survival into adolescence typical.

Other classic CMD types: Ullrich CMD (collagen VI — COL6A1/A2/A3): rigid spine + distal joint hyperextensibility; CMD with rigid spine (SEPN1/selenoprotein N, chromosome 1p36): early spine rigidity + restrictive lung disease; SEPN1 mutations also cause multi-minicore disease; function unknown; MDC1C (fukutin-related protein, FKRP, chr 19q13) → secondary merosin deficiency + limb-girdle dystrophy in older children
6. Congenital Myotonic Dystrophy
6.1 Genetics and Pathogenesis

DM1: autosomal dominant; CTG trinucleotide repeat expansion in DMPK gene (myotonic dystrophy protein kinase) on chromosome 19q13.3; >45 repeats = disease expression; congenital form = >1000 CTG repeats
DM2: CCTG tetranucleotide expansion in ZNF9 gene (chromosome 3q21); does NOT present in congenital form

Genetic anticipation: DM1 has progressively earlier onset in successive generations; larger repeat expansions during oogenesis (>1000) than spermatogenesis → congenital form almost ALWAYS inherited from MOTHER; rarely from father (oligospermia also present in males)
Pathomechanism: nuclear accumulation of abnormal mRNA from large repeat expansion → delay in differentiation and maturation of skeletal muscle
Incidence: ≥1/3500 live births for congenital DM1
6.2 Clinical Presentation

Congenital Myotonic Dystrophy — Clinical Hallmarks
Antenatal clues: polyhydramnios (failure of fetal swallowing); ↓ fetal movements; malpresentation; premature labor; prolonged labor + postpartum hemorrhage (poor uterine contractions). Neonatal features: generalized weakness + profound hypotonia; facial diplegia (key finding); tent-shaped triangular upper lip (pathognomonic); absent/↓ DTRs; congenital talipes (clubfoot); joint contractures; respiratory distress (muscle weakness + pulmonary immaturity + ↓ central respiratory control); poor suck/swallow → aspiration risk; full eye movements but ↓ fixation/following. CK: NORMAL (contrast with CMD). Diagnosis: examine mother! — clinical + EMG examination of mother → molecular genetic analysis (CTG repeat expansion) for confirmation. EMG in infant: myopathic potentials; clinical/electrical myotonia NOT present in neonates (appears early childhood). Prognosis: many die early despite ICU; ↑ mortality if >30 days ventilation; those who survive: hypotonia improves by childhood; facial diplegia + ptosis + triangular mouth persists → characteristic adult facies; most children walk by age 3 yr; all eventually develop full adult DM spectrum; most develop MR or significant learning difficulties.
Radiographic findings: elevated right hemidiaphragm (liver + diaphragm weakness); hydrops fetalis in cardiac failure
Neuroimaging: ventriculomegaly (prenatal origin); IVH, subarachnoid hemorrhage, early PVL in preterm; WM changes on MRI
Cardiac: conduction defects common later — typically NOT presenting feature in newborn (monitor in follow-up)
Feeding: common problem; multifactorial — facial muscle weakness + delayed gastric motility
7. Congenital Myopathies
7.1 Overview
Primary muscle disorders present at birth; genetically determined; differentiated by histology + histochemistry; NOT inflammatory myopathies, metabolic disorders, or inborn errors of metabolism
>30 types described; 4 classic types with unique histologic findings covered here
7.2 Nemaline Myopathy

Histology: rod bodies (nemaline rods) = threadlike structures on Gomori modified trichrome stain (red against blue-green background); subsarcolemmal; intranuclear rods in severe neonatal form; originate from Z-discs (alpha-actinin, actin); type 1 fiber predominance + small size

Genetics: mutations in 7 thin filament genes: TPM3 (alpha-tropomyosin), TPM2, ACTA1 (alpha-actin), NEB (nebulin), TNNT1 (troponin T1 — Amish population, fatal in 2nd yr), CFL2, KBTBD13; most cases sporadic; AD or AR

Clinical spectrum: severe congenital form: generalized weakness + hypotonia including face + bulbar + respiratory; eye muscles SPARED; mild to moderate forms with facioscapuloperoneal pattern; relatively nonprogressive; long thin face + arched palate + poor muscle bulk; preferential diaphragmatic weakness in all variants — % requiring prolonged ventilation is significant
Severity predictor: respiratory involvement at delivery → determines survival and motor outcome in neonatal form; CK normal/slightly ↑; cardiac involvement rare (secondary to RHF/pulmonary HTN)
7.3 Central Core Disease
Autosomal dominant: central cores of degenerated myofibrils in type 1 fibers (which predominate); cores lack oxidative activity
Genetics: RYR1 gene (ryanodine receptor) on chromosome 19q12-13.2 (~80% of cases); closely linked to malignant hyperthermia susceptibility locus

DANGER — Central Core Disease + Malignant Hyperthermia
ALL patients with central core disease are at risk for malignant hyperthermia (MH)! RYR1 gene encodes ryanodine receptor → abnormal Ca²⁺ release from SR → triggered by volatile anesthetic agents and succinylcholine → life-threatening hypermetabolic crisis. Alert anesthesia team BEFORE any surgery. Use TIVA (total intravenous anesthesia). Have dantrolene available. Mild presentation in neonatal period does NOT predict MH severity. This is arguably the most important safety pearl in all of neonatal neuromuscular disease.
Clinical: usually mild at neonatal presentation; proximal muscle weakness + hypotonia; congenital hip dislocation common; mild motor delay; scoliosis + contractures develop later
7.4 Multi-Minicore Myopathy
Histology: multiple small areas of sarcomeric disorganization lacking oxidative activity; predominance of small type 1 fibers
Genetics: SEPN1 (selenoprotein N) or RYR1 gene; AR or sporadic; selenoprotein N also causes rigid spine CMD (Section 5.2)

4 phenotypes: (1) classic: proximal weakness + joint laxity + scoliosis + respiratory insufficiency with rigid spine; (2) ophthalmoplegic form; (3) early onset → arthrogryposis; (4) slowly progressive with hand amyotrophy; cardiomyopathy may occur
7.5 Myotubular Myopathy (X-Linked)

Most severe congenital myopathy: X-linked recessive; MTM1 gene on chromosome Xq28; protein tyrosine phosphorylase → vital role in normal myogenesis; muscle fibers contain large central nuclei resembling fetal myotubes (persistence of fetal cytoskeletal proteins vimentin + desmin)
Antenatal: polyhydramnios; often dead or critically ill at birth or within first year from respiratory failure; long-term survival possible
Clinical: generalized hypotonia + facial weakness + ophthalmoplegia; congenital contractures; nonprogressive in survivors but extremely weak — cannot sit unsupported
Diagnosis: muscle biopsy; genetic analysis of MTM1 gene available (prenatal diagnosis possible); CK normal; EMG not helpful
Female heterozygotes: may have early-onset limb-girdle weakness but usually asymptomatic
8. Metabolic and Mitochondrial Disorders
8.1 Mitochondrial Myopathies
Categories: (1) substrate transport defects; (2) substrate utilization/citric acid cycle defects; (3) electron transport chain (ETC) + oxidative phosphorylation defects
Neonatal ETC defects: most likely to cause prominent muscle disease in newborn; cytochrome-c oxidase (COX) deficiency = most common presenting as neonatal myopathy

COX deficiency — severe form: profound weakness + hypotonia + hyporeflexia + poor feeding + respiratory difficulty + lactic acidosis; hepatomegaly + cardiomyopathy + renal tubular defects (de Toni-Fanconi syndrome) + macroglossia; death within months

COX deficiency — reversible form: similar presentation but biochemically and clinically improves over 1–2 years; differentiated by immunohistochemistry (fatal form: absent subunit VIIa,b; benign form: absent subunit VIIa,b AND absent subunit II); early diagnosis → support while improvement occurs
Leigh syndrome: COX deficiency + encephalopathy + MRI changes (bilateral basal ganglia/brainstem); associated with SURF1 gene mutations (chr 9q34)
Muscle biopsy: nonspecific myopathic changes; ragged red fibers (Gomori trichrome); absent COX histochemical staining; EM: ↑ abnormal mitochondria + lipid + glycogen
8.2 Pompe Disease (GSD II — Acid Maltase Deficiency)
Deficiency of acid alpha-glucosidase (GAA gene, chromosome 17q23-25): autosomal recessive; glycogen accumulation in anterior horn cells + muscle + heart + liver + brain

Clinical (infantile form): profound generalized weakness + hypotonia + hyporeflexia + heart failure + hepatomegaly; tongue fasciculations + macroglossia + severe bulbar weakness; CXR: cardiomegaly; ECG: short PR intervals + giant QRS complexes — characteristic!
Labs: ↑ CK + ↑ liver enzymes; EMG: fibrillations + small polyphasic potentials; biopsy: glycogen vacuoles (PAS+)
Diagnosis: enzyme deficiency in leukocytes/fibroblasts/muscle or mutations in both GAA alleles; common mutations in Ashkenazi Jews + Amish

Treatment: enzyme replacement therapy (ERT) with alglucosidase alfa — can delay need for ventilator + cardiac morbidity but response highly variable; poor prognosis without treatment (most die within 6 months)
Newborn screening: now available in many states — early detection + treatment critical for best outcomes
8.3 Other Glycogen Storage Diseases

McArdle disease (GSD V — phosphorylase deficiency, chr 11p13): typically adolescent onset; rare neonatal form: severe weakness + contractures + feeding difficulties + ↓ phosphorylase staining on biopsy + subsarcolemmal glycogen (non-membrane bound); slow progressive weakness without cranial nerve involvement; some fatal in neonatal period

Phosphofructokinase deficiency (GSD VII): severe infantile form: hypotonia + progressive weakness + contractures; ± seizures + cortical blindness + MR; non-membrane-bound subsarcolemmal glycogen on biopsy; treatment limited
Debrancher enzyme deficiency (GSD III): rare neonatal presentation: hypotonia + weakness + hypoglycemia + hepatomegaly + liver dysfunction
8.4 Primary Carnitine Deficiency
OCTN2 (carnitine transporter) defect — SLC22A5 gene: typically early childhood (cardiomyopathy + proximal weakness + encephalopathy); rare neonatal form: weakness + hypotonia + cardiomyopathy
Key clue: hypoketotic hypoglycemia; muscle biopsy: lipid-containing vacuoles; ↓ serum + muscle carnitine; transporter defect in cultured fibroblasts
8.5 Peroxisomal Disorders

Zellweger syndrome (cerebro-hepato-renal): PEX gene mutations; absent functional peroxisomes; profound hypotonia + weakness ± hyporeflexia/areflexia; facial dysmorphism; hepatomegaly; cataracts; retinopathy; calcific stippling of epiphyses; rhizomelia; heterotopic neurons in neocortex + cerebellum; death within first postnatal months
Mechanism: PAF synthesis deficit → NMDA receptor dysfunction → abnormal Ca²⁺ mobilization → migration defect
9. NICU Practical — Floppy Infant Workup
9.1 Stepwise Investigation Ladder

Floppy Infant Workup — Systematic Approach
Step 1 — Rule out systemic/metabolic causes FIRST: sepsis workup; blood glucose, Ca, Mg, electrolytes; organ function (LFT, RFT); maternal medication history (MgSO4, aminoglycosides, benzos). Step 2 — Localize: central vs peripheral: DTR character; tongue fasciculations; facial diplegia; eye movements; antigravity power; cortical thumb; primitive reflexes; brain imaging if central suspected. Step 3 — Focused investigation for peripheral hypotonia: CK (myopathy → ↑↑; SMA/neuropathy → normal); NCS/EMG (anterior horn cell vs neuropathy vs NMJ vs myopathy — each has characteristic pattern); maternal examination + serum AChR antibodies (NMJ suspected); genetic panel (chromosomal microarray, whole exome/genome sequencing). Step 4 — Muscle biopsy (if genetic tests inconclusive): histology + histochemistry + EM + immunocytochemistry; becoming less common with advances in molecular genetics. Step 5 — Metabolic: lactate + pyruvate (mitochondria); VLCFA (peroxisomal); lysosomal enzyme panels; urine organic acids; plasma amino acids; acylcarnitine profile (Pompe, carnitine deficiency).
9.2 EMG/NCS Pattern Recognition
9.3 Maternal History Checklist
MG diagnosis (active, in remission, undiagnosed) — AChR Ab titer; duration of disease (longer duration = lower neonatal MG risk)
Myotonic dystrophy — examine mother: myotonia on percussion, grip myotonia, temporal wasting; maternal CTG repeat size predicts congenital form risk
MgSO4 administration + dose + timing; aminoglycoside use; benzodiazepines/opioids
Family history of neuromuscular disease; consanguinity (↑ AR conditions); ethnicity (Ashkenazi → familial dysautonomia; Japanese → Fukuyama CMD; Amish → nemaline TNNT1 mutation)
Polyhydramnios + ↓ fetal movement = fetal akinesia → neuromuscular disorder until proven otherwise
9.4 Specific NICU Management Points

Myotonic dystrophy — ventilator management: if >30 days ventilation → ↑ mortality; but diaphragmatic maturation occurs with time → most can wean eventually; document CTG repeat size; anticipate prolonged NICU stay; coordinate with genetics team

SMA type 0/I — treatment urgency: gene therapy most effective when treated EARLIEST (presymptomatic); if SMA suspected → expedited genetic testing; involve neurology/genetics immediately; family counseling + ethics discussion for severe cases
Neonatal MG — feeding management: small frequent tube feedings; neostigmine 30 min before feeds; monitor for cholinergic excess (diarrhea, secretions, fasciculations); no edrophonium

Infantile botulism: do NOT give cholinesterase inhibitors (ineffective); do NOT give antibiotics that affect intestinal flora unless systemic infection; BabyBIG is the treatment; stool culture for C. botulinum
Central core disease — anesthesia flag: document RYR1 mutation in medical record; alert all surgery teams; avoid succinylcholine + volatile agents; dantrolene available
10. 5 Bedside Pearls

PEARL 1 — SMA: 3 Drugs, Screen Everywhere — Treat Presymptomatically
SMA type I = ประวัติศาสตร์ที่เปลี่ยนไปแล้ว: ก่อน 2017 = fatal before 1 yr; ปัจจุบัน 3 FDA-approved therapies (nusinersen 2017, onasemnogene abeparvovec 2020, risdiplam 2021) พิสูจน์แล้วว่า earlier treatment = dramatically better outcomes. Newborn screening ใน 44 states identifies SMA before symptoms → presymptomatic treatment → some babies walk. NICU key action: ถ้าเห็น floppy infant ที่ alert, absent reflexes, tongue fasciculations, bell-shaped chest, normal CK → SMA first until proven otherwise → expedited genetic testing → consult neurology STAT → discuss treatment eligibility immediately.

PEARL 2 — Congenital Myotonic Dystrophy: ALWAYS Examine the Mother
Floppy infant + facial diplegia + tent-shaped triangular upper lip → examine mother BEFORE ordering genetic panel on baby. แม่ที่ mild/in remission/undiagnosed DM1 ยังสามารถให้กำเนิดทารกที่ severe congenital DM1 ได้ เพราะ oogenesis → ↑ CTG repeat expansion (>1000). กล้ามมือแม่ myotonia on percussion / grip? Temporal wasting? แม่อาจไม่ทราบว่าตัวเองมี DM. ถ้าแม่ positive → confirm CTG size → ทารกได้รับ counseling ที่ถูกต้องตั้งแต่ต้น. CK ปกติใน congenital DM (contrast with CMD).

PEARL 3 — Central Core Disease = Malignant Hyperthermia Risk in Every Surgery
Central core disease ดูไม่ scary ใน NICU — mild proximal weakness, congenital hip dislocation, motor delay. แต่ RYR1 gene mutation = ↑ risk malignant hyperthermia ทุก surgery ตลอดชีวิต. หน้าที่เรา: (1) document RYR1 mutation ใน problem list + allergy list; (2) alert ทุก surgical team; (3) ห้าม succinylcholine + volatile anesthetic agents; (4) TIVA only; (5) dantrolene ต้องพร้อมใช้. ถ้า neonates ที่มี congenital myopathy ต้องผ่าตัด → ทำ workup gene ก่อน ถ้าเป็นไปได้.

PEARL 4 — Transient Neonatal MG: DTRs NORMAL; Neostigmine Not Edrophonium
Transient neonatal MG: เกิดในทารกของแม่ที่มี MG (แม้ in remission); onset 0–72 hr; 20% incidence; generalized weakness + bulbar weakness + respiratory failure. Key distinguisher: DTRs NORMAL (absent ใน SMA, neuropathy, severe myopathy). Diagnosis: neostigmine IM 0.04 mg/kg → objective improvement ใน 15 min (ventilation, oxygenation, sucking) — ห้ามใช้ edrophonium (risk respiratory arrest in neonates). Treatment 4 weeks average; 90% recover within 2 months. ถ้าไม่รู้ → อาจ miss diagnosis → ทารก deteriorate.

PEARL 5 — Infantile Botulism: C. botulinum Colonizes Gut (NOT Exotoxin) → BabyBIG
Infantile botulism ≠ food-borne botulism. ทารก 2 wk–6 mo: C. botulinum colonizes gut → produces toxin IN SITU → presynaptic cholinergic blockage. Classic presentation: constipation ก่อน → poor feeding → descending flaccid weakness → loss of DTRs + cranial nerve dysfunction (pupil dilation + ptosis). EMG hallmark: incremental response at high-frequency (20–50 Hz) stimulation (contrast with decrement ใน MG). Stool culture confirms. Treatment: BabyBIG (human-derived botulism Ig) → ↓ duration significantly. ห้ามให้ cholinesterase inhibitors; ห้ามให้ antibiotics ที่กระทบ gut flora (worsen by releasing more toxin from lysed bacteria) เว้นแต่ systemic infection.
11. Evidence Update — Key Studies

Finkel RS et al NEJM 2017;377:1723: ENDEAR trial — nusinersen vs sham in infantile-onset SMA: 51% vs 0% motor-milestone response; significant event-free survival benefit — established nusinersen as first SMA treatment

Mendell JR et al NEJM 2017;377:1713: single-dose gene replacement therapy (onasemnogene) for SMA: patients treated earliest showed most improvement; some achieved ability to walk — established gene therapy for SMA
Darras BT et al NEJM 2021;385:427: FIREFISH — risdiplam-treated SMA type I infants vs historical controls: superior motor milestones; established oral SMN-splicing modifier for SMA
Jdrzejowska M Degener Neurol 2020;10:39: SMA newborn screening: identifies 364 babies, prevents ~30 deaths, 50 infants avoid assisted ventilation — epidemiologic basis for NBS programs

Kochhar PK et al J Perinatol 2021;41:2279: transient neonatal MG risk estimate: 20% of infants born to MG mothers develop symptoms; inverse relationship between maternal disease duration and neonatal MG incidence
Rao AK et al MMWR 2021;70:1: clinical guidelines for botulism diagnosis and treatment 2021: BabyBIG for infantile botulism reduces hospitalization duration; comprehensive management guidelines

Engel AG et al Lancet Neurol 2015;14:420: congenital myasthenic syndromes pathogenesis, diagnosis, treatment: comprehensive review; endplate AChE deficiency worsened by cholinesterase inhibitors — critical for safe management
Butterfield RJ Continuum 2019;25:1640: congenital muscular dystrophy and congenital myopathy review: current classification by protein defect + immunocytochemistry/DNA; practical diagnostic approach
Sarkozy A et al Front Mol Neurosci 2020;13:123: LAMA2-related dystrophies: clinical phenotypes, disease biomarkers, clinical trial readiness — white matter changes on MRI important diagnostic finding
Johnson NE Continuum 2019;25:1682: myotonic muscular dystrophies: DM1 vs DM2 clinical comparison; congenital DM1 = maternal inheritance + >1000 CTG repeats; anticipation mechanism

Lanni S, Pearson CE Neurobiol Dis 2019;132:104533: molecular genetics of congenital myotonic dystrophy: somatic instability of CTG repeat + oogenesis expansion → almost exclusive maternal transmission; detailed mechanism review

Zapata-Aldana E et al J Neuromuscul Dis 2018;5:331: prenatal, neonatal, early childhood features in congenital myotonic dystrophy: polyhydramnios + malpresentation common; poor feeding multifactorial (facial weakness + ↓ gastric motility)

Ogasawara M, Nishino I Neuromuscul Disord 2021;31:968: review of core myopathies: central core disease, multiminicore, dusty core, core-rod myopathy — RYR1 is the major gene; overlap with MH discussed

Laitila J, Wallgren-Pettersson C Neuromuscul Disord 2021;31:955: recent advances in nemaline myopathy: 7-gene panel; respiratory involvement predicts neonatal survival; intranuclear rods in severe neonatal form

Bay LB et al Arch Argent Pediatr 2019;117:271: infantile Pompe disease diagnosis and management: ERT (alglucosidase alfa) delays ventilator need and cardiac morbidity but variable response; early diagnosis critical

Trang H et al Orphanet J Rare Dis 2020;15:252: guidelines for congenital central hypoventilation syndrome: PHOX2B mutation; hypoventilation without response to hypercarbia; Hirschsprung association; management algorithm

Tarnopolsky MA Continuum 2016;22:1829: metabolic myopathies including mitochondrial: cytochrome-c oxidase deficiency = most common neonatal mitochondrial myopathy; reversible vs fatal forms differentiated by immunohistochemistry; lactic acidosis ± multisystem features

Numoto S et al Pediatr Int 2022;64:e14747: effects of maternal MgSO4 on newborns: hypotonia, areflexia, respiratory depression — dose-dependent; worsened by hypocalcemia; management: Ca gluconate + supportive care
12. Self-Check Q&A
Q: Central vs peripheral hypotonia — ความต่างที่สำคัญที่สุด 3 ข้อ?

ตอบ: (1) Tone vs weakness ratio: central = tone ↓ disproportionately more than weakness (antigravity power preserved); peripheral = profound weakness ≈ tone ↓. (2) DTRs: central = normal/↑/clonus; peripheral = ↓ or absent. (3) Specific features: central: cortical thumb fisting, ↓ arousal, seizures, abnormal eye movements; peripheral: tongue fasciculations (anterior horn), facial diplegia (NMJ/myopathy), ↑ CK (myopathy). Caveat: near-total asphyxia = absent DTRs despite central origin; CMD + congenital myotonic dystrophy can have both.
Q: SMA type I: phenotype และ 3 FDA-approved drugs — mechanism ของแต่ละตัว?

ตอบ: Phenotype: symmetric flaccid paralysis proximal > distal, lower > upper; absent DTRs; tongue fasciculations; alert with normal eyes; bell-shaped chest (intercostal weak, diaphragm spared); cardiac unaffected; CK normal. 1. Nusinersen: antisense oligonucleotide; modifies SMN2 pre-mRNA splicing → ↑ full-length SMN protein; intrathecal. 2. Onasemnogene: AAV vector single IV infusion → delivers SMN1 gene; gene replacement. 3. Risdiplam: oral small molecule; modifies SMN2 pre-mRNA splicing → ↑ SMN protein. Earlier treatment = better outcome.
Q: Transient neonatal MG vs congenital myasthenic syndrome — ความต่างในการวินิจฉัยและรักษา?

ตอบ: Transient neonatal MG: acquired (maternal IgG transfer); resolves within 2 months; maternal AChR antibody positive; neostigmine diagnostic + therapeutic; DTRs normal. CMS: genetic; antibody NEGATIVE; permanent (though may improve with age); episodic apnea risk; endplate AChE deficiency worsened by cholinesterase inhibitors (↑ must screen subtype before treating). Both: decremental response at 2 Hz repetitive nerve stimulation (not botulism which is incremental at high freq).
Q: Congenital myotonic dystrophy: genetics และ ทำไม maternal transmission?

ตอบ: DM1: CTG repeat expansion in DMPK gene (chr 19q13.3); congenital form = >1000 CTG repeats. Maternal transmission mechanism: (1) oogenesis → larger repeat expansions (>1000) than spermatogenesis; (2) oligospermia in affected males. Genetic anticipation: earlier onset in successive generations. Clinical hallmarks: polyhydramnios + ↓ fetal movements + premature labor → facial diplegia + tent-shaped triangular upper lip + absent DTRs + congenital talipes; CK NORMAL; examine mother for grip myotonia + temporal wasting!
Q: Central core disease: ทำไมถึง dangerous สำหรับ anesthesia และ management คือ?

ตอบ: RYR1 gene (chr 19q12-13.2): encodes ryanodine receptor → abnormal SR Ca²⁺ release channel; closely linked to malignant hyperthermia susceptibility locus. MH trigger: volatile anesthetics (halothane, isoflurane, sevoflurane) + succinylcholine → uncontrolled SR Ca²⁺ release → ↑ muscle metabolism → hyperthermia + rigidity + acidosis + hyperkalemia → cardiac arrest. ALL central core disease patients at risk for MH regardless of how mild the myopathy appears. Management: TIVA (propofol + opioid); NO volatile agents; NO succinylcholine; dantrolene available in OR; document RYR1 mutation in allergy list.
Q: Infantile botulism: pathogenesis และ hallmark EMG finding?

ตอบ: Pathogenesis: intestinal colonization by C. botulinum (NOT exotoxin ingestion like adult botulism) → in situ toxin production → presynaptic cholinergic blockade (blocks ACh vesicle release); affects autonomic + skeletal + smooth muscle. EMG hallmark: INCREMENTAL response at high-frequency (20–50 Hz) repetitive nerve stimulation (contrast with DECREMENTAL response in MG at low frequency); short-duration low-amplitude MUPs. Treatment: BabyBIG (human-derived botulism Ig); supportive; stool culture confirmation; do NOT give cholinesterase inhibitors.
Q: Pompe disease (GSD II): ทำไม ECG สำคัญ และ treatment คืออะไร?

ตอบ: ECG findings: short PR intervals + giant QRS complexes from glycogen accumulation in myocardium → mimics ventricular hypertrophy; CXR: cardiomegaly. Pathogenesis: deficiency of acid alpha-glucosidase (GAA gene) → lysosomal glycogen accumulates in heart + skeletal muscle + anterior horn cells + liver. Treatment: enzyme replacement therapy (alglucosidase alfa) → ↓ glycogen accumulation → delays ventilator need + ↓ cardiac morbidity; response variable; NBS allows early treatment → better outcomes.
Q: Familial dysautonomia (HSAN III): 5 cardinal criteria และ ethnic group?

ตอบ: 5 cardinal criteria (ALL must be present for clinical diagnosis): (1) absence of overflow tears; (2) absence of lingual fungiform papillae; (3) depressed patellar reflexes; (4) lack of axonal flare after intradermal histamine injection; (5) Ashkenazi Jewish extraction. Gene: IKBKAP (IkB kinase-associated protein), chromosome 9q31-q33; accounts for 99% of cases. Neonatal features: hypotonia + labile temperature/BP + breath holding + poor feeding + FTT + vomiting; clinical genetic testing available.
Q: Congenital hypomyelinating neuropathy: NCV เท่าไหร่ และ genes ที่เกี่ยวข้อง?

ตอบ: Motor NCV: markedly decreased < 10 m/s (normal ~40–60 m/s in term infant) — pathognomonic of congenital demyelinating neuropathy; ↑ CSF protein; nerve biopsy: hypomyelination ± atypical onion-bulb. Key genes: Schwann cell genes (EGR2, PMP22, MPZ, CX32) + neuronal NEFL + MTMR2. Same gene can cause CMT1/Dejerine-Sottas/congenital hypomyelinating neuropathy depending on mutation. Clinical: weak + hypotonic + areflexic ± arthrogryposis; swallowing + respiratory difficulties; extraocular movements usually normal; prognosis depends on initial weakness degree.
Q: Merosin-deficient CMD (MDC1A): clinical findings ที่ unique และ LAMA2 gene?

ตอบ: MDC1A (LAMA2 gene, chromosome 6q): ~50% of all CMD; merosin (laminin alpha-2 chain) absent from muscle + Schwann cells + cerebral blood vessels. Clinical unique features: (1) peripheral neuropathy (Schwann cell merosin absent); (2) T2 white matter changes on MRI after 6 months (diffuse ↑ T2 signal — cerebral vessel merosin absent); (3) epilepsy in 1/3 patients. Eye muscles spared. Cognition usually normal. Arthrogryposis + contractures. Diagnosis: absent merosin staining on biopsy + LAMA2 sequence analysis.
Q: Myotubular myopathy: gene, X-linked pattern, clinical severity?

ตอบ: MTM1 gene (chromosome Xq28): protein tyrosine phosphorylase → vital for myogenesis; muscle fibers contain large central nuclei resembling fetal myotubes (vimentin + desmin persistence). X-linked recessive: hemizygous males affected (severely); female heterozygotes usually asymptomatic (may have early limb-girdle weakness). Severity: most severe congenital myopathy — generalized hypotonia + facial weakness + ophthalmoplegia; many die from respiratory failure first year; survivors nonprogressive but extremely weak + cannot sit unsupported; prenatal diagnosis available by MTM1 mutation analysis.

Day 156 Preview — Fanaroff Ch.59
บทต่อไป: Chapter 59 — Disorders in Head Shape and Size. เตรียมตัว: microcephaly vs macrocephaly: definition, primary vs secondary, genetic causes (MCPH genes, lissencephalies) vs acquired (HI, infection, toxin); craniosynostosis: premature fusion of sutures — types (sagittal, coronal, metopic, lambdoid, pansynostosis), associated syndromes (Apert, Crouzon, Pfeiffer); hydrocephalus: obstructive vs communicating, post-hemorrhagic hydrocephalus in preterm (ventriculomegaly → PHVD → VP shunt timing); neonatal head ultrasound interpretation; management of increased ICP in neonates.
